task: I’m going to be away from my usual workspace for a few days and need to keep a couple of routine tasks moving. What are some practical things to line up ahead of time so I can handle small surprises without losing momentum?

turns.agent_response:
A good way to stay steady while away is to set up a few “if this, then that” safeguards before you leave.

### 1) Clarify the must-do tasks
- List the 2–5 tasks that absolutely need attention.
- Mark what can wait, what can be delegated, and what would count as an emergency.
- Note any deadlines, dependencies, or “don’t touch unless…” items.

### 2) Leave a simple decision guide
For each routine task, write:
- what to do if everything is normal,
- what to do if something is missing or late,
- when to pause and ask you,
- who to contact if you’re unavailable.

Keep it short enough that someone can use it quickly.

### 3) Pre-authorize low-risk decisions
Examples:
- approve small expenses up to a set amount,
- allow a teammate to reschedule or reorder work,
- define acceptable substitutions or workarounds.

This prevents tiny issues from stalling progress.

### 4) Put key info in one place
Make sure the right people can find:
- login/access instructions if relevant,
- file locations,
- templates or checklists,
- contact list,
- current status of each task.

A shared note or document is often enough.

### 5) Build in a check-in rhythm
- Set a daily or every-other-day update window.
- Ask for a brief status format: done / blocked / next / needs your input.
- Keep it async if possible so you’re not pulled into constant calls.

### 6) Create a “small surprises” plan
Think through common hiccups:
- missing file,
- delayed reply,
- system issue,
- unclear request,
- last-minute change.

For each, decide the fallback action so nobody has to improvise from scratch.

### 7) Reduce your first-day-back load
Before leaving:
- leave a short summary of what’s in motion,
- note what changed recently,
- list anything that will need your attention when you return.

That makes re-entry much smoother.

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page pre-departure checklist you can reuse.

user_simulator.text: Pretty close. I’d want it a little more concrete, more like a working checklist I can actually use before I leave. And since I’m usually dealing with files, deadlines, and a couple of moving parts, I’d add backup access, a contact chain, and a quick note on what to do if something comes in wrong or late. That’s the part that usually causes trouble. If you want, tighten it into a short pre-departure list with those pieces built in.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, more practical pre-departure checklist you can use before stepping away.

## Pre-departure checklist for routine work

### 1) Confirm what must move while you’re away
- List the 2–5 tasks that need attention.
- For each one, note:
  - deadline
  - owner
  - current status
  - next step
  - what “done” looks like
- Mark anything that can wait until you return.

### 2) Put backup access in place
- Make sure the right person can reach:
  - shared files/folders
  - key systems/accounts, if needed
  - templates, trackers, and reference docs
- Confirm login or access instructions are documented somewhere secure.
- Test access before you leave if possible.

### 3) Leave a simple contact chain
For each task, write:
- primary contact
- backup contact
- who to escalate to if both are unavailable
- when they should contact you vs. make a decision themselves

Keep it short and easy to scan.

### 4) Add a “if it comes in wrong or late” rule
For each routine task, define what to do if:
- a file is missing
- something arrives late
- the request is unclear
- the wrong version is sent

Example:
- If the file is late by less than 1 day, wait and continue.
- If it’s late by more than 1 day, notify X and use the last approved version.
- If the file is wrong, flag it immediately and ask Y for the corrected version.
- If the request is unclear, use the standard template and send it back for confirmation.

### 5) Pre-approve small decisions
Decide ahead of time:
- what can be changed without asking you
- what needs your approval
- any spending or timing limits
- acceptable substitutions or workarounds

### 6) Leave a status snapshot
Create one short note with:
- what’s in progress
- what’s pending
- deadlines this week
- known risks
- anything that should wait for your return

### 7) Set the update rhythm
- Ask for updates daily or every other day.
- Use a simple format:
  - done
  - blocked
  - next
  - needs your input
- Tell people when to escalate instead of waiting.

## Quick “before I go” final check
- [ ] Tasks and deadlines listed
- [ ] Backup access confirmed
- [ ] Contact chain written
- [ ] Late/wrong-item rule defined
- [ ] Decision limits set
- [ ] Status note shared
- [ ] Update rhythm agreed

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page fill-in template you can reuse every time you’re away.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Concrete enough to use, not a bunch of fluff. The late/wrong-item rule is the part I’d actually want in writing, and the backup access plus contact chain covers the usual headaches.

One small thing I’d add if I were using it for real: a line for who can make the call if the file comes in off by a little but still usable. That’s where people waste time waiting around.

But as a short pre-departure list, this works.
